card.indications: Treatment of focal-onset seizures, myoclonic seizures in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, and primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures, as monotherapy or adjunctive therapy depending on age and formulation.
card.mechanism: Binds synaptic vesicle protein SV2A and modulates neurotransmitter release, reducing abnormal neuronal synchronization.
card.dosing: Adult immediate-release therapy commonly begins at 500 mg twice daily and is titrated to response. Extended-release products are generally once daily. Renal dose adjustment is required. Pediatric dosing is weight based.
card.routeAdministration: Give consistently with or without food. Swallow extended-release tablets whole. Measure oral solution accurately. IV and oral total daily doses are generally equivalent when switching routes; infuse according to product directions.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to levetiracetam or formulation components.
card.warnings: Can cause behavioral abnormalities, psychosis, aggression, depression, and suicidal thoughts. Somnolence, fatigue, coordination difficulty, serious skin reactions, anaphylaxis, and withdrawal seizures may occur. Dose reduction is required with renal impairment.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Somnolence, fatigue, dizziness, headache, irritability, weakness, decreased appetite, and infection-like symptoms in children.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Suicidal behavior, psychosis, severe aggression, anaphylaxis, angioedema, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, blood dyscrasias, and increased seizures after abrupt discontinuation.
card.interactions: It has relatively few clinically important metabolic interactions. Other CNS depressants can increase sedation and impaired coordination.
card.monitoring: Monitor seizure frequency, renal function, mental status, mood, aggression, suicidal thinking, sedation, coordination, CBC when clinically indicated, and adherence.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly. Report major mood or behavior changes, suicidal thoughts, rash, facial swelling, or worsening seizures. Avoid driving until effects are known and measure liquid doses carefully.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration and regular meals. Avoid alcohol if it increases sedation or seizure risk.

card.indications: Replacement therapy for congenital or acquired hypothyroidism and, in selected patients, suppression of thyroid-stimulating hormone for thyroid cancer or nodular disease.
card.mechanism: Synthetic thyroxine (T4) is converted in tissues to triiodothyronine (T3), which binds nuclear receptors and regulates metabolism, growth, and development.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by age, weight, cardiac status, pregnancy, severity and duration of hypothyroidism, and TSH response. Healthy adults are often dosed near 1.6 mcg/kg/day, while older adults or patients with coronary disease start much lower. Adjustments are usually made in 12.5- to 25-mcg increments after appropriate laboratory intervals.
card.routeAdministration: Give on an empty stomach 30 to 60 minutes before breakfast with water, or consistently at bedtime at least 3 to 4 hours after food. Separate calcium, iron, aluminum antacids, bile-acid sequestrants, and certain other products by at least 4 hours. Keep the same formulation when possible.
card.contraindications: Uncorrected adrenal insufficiency and hypersensitivity to product components. Use caution with untreated thyrotoxicosis and significant cardiovascular disease.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: thyroid hormone must not be used for obesity or weight loss. Excess replacement can cause angina, arrhythmia, myocardial infarction, bone loss, and symptoms of thyrotoxicosis. Inadequate treatment in pregnancy can harm the fetus.
card.commonAdverseEffects: When the dose is excessive: palpitations, tremor, anxiety, sweating, heat intolerance, diarrhea, insomnia, headache, and weight loss.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Atrial fibrillation, angina, myocardial infarction, severe hypertension, heart failure, seizures, adrenal crisis when insufficiency is untreated, and reduced bone density with chronic overreplacement.
card.interactions: Calcium, iron, antacids, sucralfate, bile-acid sequestrants, and some foods reduce absorption. Warfarin effect may increase. Diabetes medication needs may change. Proton-pump inhibitors and enzyme-inducing drugs can alter requirements.
card.monitoring: Monitor TSH and free T4 at recommended intervals, symptoms, pulse, weight, cardiac status, growth in children, bone risk with long-term suppression, and adherence or formulation changes.
card.patientTeaching: Take the same way every day and do not switch brands or stop without guidance. Improvement may take several weeks. Report chest pain, rapid or irregular pulse, severe nervousness, or tremor. This medication is not a weight-loss drug.
card.nutrition: Take fasting with water and separate calcium, iron, soy, high-fiber supplements, and enteral feedings as directed. Maintain a consistent diet rather than making abrupt changes.

card.indications: Local or regional anesthesia, topical anesthesia, and treatment of selected serious ventricular arrhythmias with IV formulations. Indications and maximum doses vary by concentration, route, and whether epinephrine is included.
card.mechanism: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. In cardiac tissue it suppresses ventricular automaticity and conduction.
card.dosing: Local-anesthetic dosing is based on procedure, site, patient weight, concentration, and epinephrine use. IV antiarrhythmic therapy uses a loading dose followed by a monitored infusion. Maximum cumulative dose must be calculated carefully across all lidocaine-containing products.
card.routeAdministration: Confirm concentration and route before use. Aspirate before injections when appropriate to reduce intravascular administration. Use continuous ECG and blood-pressure monitoring for IV antiarrhythmic therapy. Apply patches only to intact skin and follow labeled on/off time.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to amide-type local anesthetics. Certain IV antiarrhythmic uses are contraindicated in severe sinoatrial, atrioventricular, or intraventricular block without a pacemaker. Do not apply topical products to severely damaged skin unless specifically directed.
card.warnings: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity can cause seizures, respiratory arrest, hypotension, and fatal arrhythmias. Risk rises with accidental intravascular injection, excessive cumulative dosing, hepatic impairment, low cardiac output, or use of multiple lidocaine products.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Local numbness or irritation, dizziness, drowsiness, tinnitus, metallic taste, nausea, and mild blood-pressure changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Seizures, coma, respiratory arrest, severe hypotension, bradycardia, heart block, ventricular arrhythmias, cardiac arrest, methemoglobinemia, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Other local anesthetics and class I antiarrhythmics add toxicity. Amiodarone, beta blockers, cimetidine, and impaired hepatic blood flow may increase exposure. CNS depressants can worsen sedation.
card.monitoring: Monitor total cumulative dose, neurologic symptoms, respiratory status, blood pressure, pulse, ECG with IV therapy, injection site, skin integrity with patches, and signs of methemoglobinemia.
card.patientTeaching: Do not use extra lidocaine products without approval. Protect numb areas from heat, biting, or injury. Remove patches according to schedule. Seek emergency help for ringing ears with dizziness, metallic taste, severe confusion, seizure, blue or gray skin, or palpitations.
card.nutrition: After oral or throat anesthesia, avoid food and hot liquids until swallowing and sensation return to prevent aspiration or burns. No routine dietary restrictions apply.

card.indications: Treatment of selected susceptible gram-positive infections, including certain pneumonias, complicated skin infections, and infections caused by vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium. It does not treat gram-negative infections.
card.mechanism: Binds to the bacterial 23S ribosomal RNA of the 50S subunit and prevents formation of the functional 70S initiation complex, inhibiting protein synthesis.
card.dosing: Adults commonly receive 600 mg every 12 hours for many serious indications. Some uncomplicated skin infections use lower dosing. Duration depends on infection; prolonged therapy increases hematologic and neurologic toxicity. No routine renal dose adjustment is required, but metabolites accumulate in severe renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Oral and IV dosing are generally equivalent, so no dose change is usually needed when switching routes. May be taken with or without food. Gently invert reconstituted suspension rather than shaking vigorously. Review serotonergic and adrenergic medications before starting.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Avoid use with current or recent monoamine oxidase inhibitors and avoid uncontrolled interactions with serotonergic or adrenergic drugs unless the risk is specifically managed.
card.warnings: Can cause myelosuppression, serotonin syndrome, peripheral and optic neuropathy, lactic acidosis, hypoglycemia, seizures, C. difficile diarrhea, and increased mortality in certain catheter-related bloodstream infections with gram-negative pathogens.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, headache, vomiting, dizziness, altered taste, and mild blood-count changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Thrombocytopenia, pancytopenia, severe anemia, serotonin syndrome, optic or peripheral neuropathy, lactic acidosis, severe hypoglycemia, seizures, anaphylaxis, and C. difficile colitis.
card.interactions: SSRIs, SNRIs, tricyclics, buspirone, certain opioids, and other serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk. Sympathomimetics and high tyramine intake can raise blood pressure. Drugs that suppress bone marrow add hematologic risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor CBC weekly, infection response, blood pressure, temperature, neurologic symptoms, vision with prolonged therapy, glucose in patients with diabetes, lactate or bicarbonate if unexplained acidosis occurs, and bowel pattern.
card.patientTeaching: Report unusual bruising, bleeding, fever, severe diarrhea, vision changes, numbness, severe headache, agitation, sweating, tremor, or muscle rigidity. Review all antidepressants, pain medicines, cold products, and supplements with the prescriber.
card.nutrition: Avoid large amounts of high-tyramine foods such as aged cheeses, cured meats, fermented products, tap beer, and concentrated yeast extracts during therapy. May be taken with food.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension, adjunctive treatment of heart failure, and improvement of survival after acute myocardial infarction in hemodynamically stable patients.
card.mechanism: Inhibits conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion and increasing bradykinin.
card.dosing: Hypertension commonly begins at 10 mg once daily, or 5 mg when a diuretic is being used. Heart-failure and post-MI regimens start lower and titrate to response. Reduce the starting dose in renal impairment and monitor closely.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily at a consistent time with or without food. Check blood pressure, renal function, and potassium. Correct significant volume depletion when possible before initiation. Hold and notify the prescriber for pregnancy or angioedema.
card.contraindications: Pregnancy, history of ACE-inhibitor-related angioedema, hereditary or idiopathic angioedema, concurrent aliskiren use in patients with diabetes, and use within 36 hours of sacubitril/valsartan.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: fetal toxicity. Other serious risks include angioedema, severe hypotension, acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, and rare hepatic failure. Angioedema can occur at any time and may obstruct the airway.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry cough, dizziness, headache, fatigue, hypotension, increased creatinine, and increased potassium.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Airway angioedema, renal failure, severe hyperkalemia, syncope, neutropenia, hepatic failure, and fetal injury or death.
card.interactions: Potassium supplements, potassium-sparing diuretics, salt substitutes, and trimethoprim increase hyperkalemia. NSAIDs can reduce effect and worsen renal function. Diuretics increase first-dose hypotension. Lithium concentrations may rise.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, renal function, potassium, volume status, cough, facial or tongue swelling, heart-failure symptoms, and pregnancy status when relevant.
card.patientTeaching: Do not use during pregnancy. Seek emergency care for swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat. Rise slowly, report reduced urine or muscle weakness, and avoid potassium supplements or salt substitutes unless prescribed.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid potassium-containing salt substitutes and unapproved potassium supplements. Follow prescribed sodium and fluid guidance.
